gist: Ceresna and Muir argue the June 2026 selloff has dragged the S&P to a make-or-break test of its 50-day MA (~7,300-7,500), with the SpaceX/Google/Meta equity-issuance wave draining unprecedented cash just as semiconductors near mean-reversion. They are contrarian bullish on oil into a tightening physical market, bearish-but-early on gold/uranium, and lean toward selling rips over buying dips while watching for a sector rotation into financials, healthcare and staples.

themes.theme: S&P inflection: 50-day test and systematic-selling cliff

insights.claim: The S&P has wiped out a ~300-point bullish cushion fast and is now testing its 50-day moving average near 7,300-7,500, the level that determines whether this is a routine 5% correction or the start of a systematic-driven cascade.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: A hold above ~7,500 by Friday/Monday signals dip-buyers are intact; a break below ~7,300 risks triggering CTA/vol-control selling and acceleration lower.
insights.quote: We are at the edge of that cliff and it's really interesting.

insights.claim: Bank of America estimates roughly $150 billion of potential systematic CTA/risk-parity/vol-control selling on a down market, and Goldman cites ~7,300 on the S&P as where CTA sell triggers begin.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Defines the downside air-pocket level traders should watch; a break of 7,300 could unleash up to $150B of mechanical supply.

insights.claim: Sell-side CTA trigger levels are essentially marketing and unknowable because systematic flows are path-dependent, so traders should not treat the $150B/7,300 figures as precise.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: Cautions against trading off a single round-number trigger; the reaction at the level matters more than the level itself.
insights.quote: I think all those are like just marketing for the sell side, right? We don't really know where the CTAs are going to sell.

insights.claim: Every rally during the selloff has been met with active distribution, with intraday bounces failing precisely at 50% and 61.8% Fibonacci retracements and the 50-hour moving average.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Persistent failure at fib/MA levels supports a sell-the-rip posture until a rally cleanly follows through to the upside.
insights.quote: The moment there's a reaction off the low, it's as distribution kicking right in.

insights.claim: The trading playbook has flipped from buy-the-dip-then-sell-the-rip to sell-the-rip-then-buy-the-dip, meaning traders should now err short rather than long.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: A regime-change call: position bias should shift to selling strength after Friday's reversal.
insights.quote: It's now time to sell the rips and buy the dips and do it in that order.

insights.claim: Markets do not crash from all-time highs; a more likely path is a bounce back toward the highs, chopping, then drifting lower rather than an immediate decisive break.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: Tempers the urgency of the 50-day test; favors fading rallies into a topping process rather than betting on an immediate crash.
insights.quote: You don't crash ever from like all-time highs.

themes.theme: Semiconductor mean-reversion as the index tell

insights.claim: Semiconductors (SMH down only ~2.26% on a sharp down day) are holding up far better than expected given their parabolic run and high daily volatility, which argues against an outright bearish index view.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: If chips are not leading down, a 10%+ S&P correction is unlikely; watch SMH for a break to lower lows as the real bearish trigger.
insights.quote: The semiconductors should be leading the way down here if this was the real deal selloff and they're not at least not yet.

insights.claim: Semiconductors carried the bull market on their own while the Mag 7 lagged, and the Mag 7 ex-Nvidia would have been a profitable short.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: Leadership has narrowed to chips/Nvidia; the rest of mega-cap is a weak underbelly and Muir's main short.
insights.quote: That's been my main short... if you taken out Nvidia, it would have been a beautiful short.

insights.claim: The most probable next move is a sector-rotation story driven by PMs trimming bloated semiconductor overweights (e.g. a 5% AMD position that ballooned to 10%) back to neutral, rather than a correlation-1 puke across everything.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Implies rotation winners (financials, healthcare, staples) absorb chip proceeds; favors pair trades over outright index shorts.

themes.theme: IPO/secondary supply drain as a liquidity headwind

insights.claim: The SpaceX IPO is the largest in history, roughly double the size of Saudi Aramco, and combined with other deals is pulling $30B+ of cash from markets versus the entire record 2021 IPO year of only ~$120B.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: An unprecedented cash drain into a richly valued market is an underappreciated near-term headwind for equity prices.
insights.quote: We're talking unprecedented levels of money drain that they're trying to attain to pull out.

insights.claim: Google is raising ~$80B in a secondary (including a ~$10B block to Buffett's successor plus ongoing ATM sales), and Meta is also issuing, reversing years of mega-cap buybacks into net supply.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Mega-caps were valued on shrinking float; flipping from buyer to seller of their own stock removes a key bid and is a classic top signal.
insights.quote: Google has been buying back their shares for years and years and years and all of a sudden they're selling wax of them and at top dollar.

insights.claim: Companies insiders sell when valuations are full ('when the ducks quack, you feed them'), and simultaneous secondaries across Google, Meta and even SMCI (issuing to pay for inventory) are a broad top signal, not a one-off.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Treat the cluster of secondaries as smart-money distribution; watch what insiders do (issue) not what they announce (buybacks).
insights.quote: When the ducks quack, you feed them.

insights.claim: Mega-caps have already exhausted debt markets (Google issuing in Canadian dollars, euros, and via SPVs) before turning to equity, signaling they are scraping every available liquidity source.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: Reaching for every funding channel mirrors late-cycle 'rails tripping over each other for scarce capital'—a historical bearish tell.
insights.quote: They've been hitting every bid they can out there.

insights.claim: Fast-tracking NASDAQ index inclusion for new mega-issues passes the bag to passive index buyers, though indexing is still well under 50% of market cap so fundamental buyers remain the majority.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the 'indexers absorb everything' narrative; the supply-demand flip matters more than index mechanics.

themes.theme: Oil: abandoned bull case into a tightening physical market

insights.claim: Almost everyone has given up on the oil bull case, leaving the market positioned for nothing but a Trump-brokered Middle East deal, which sets up a violent upside surprise if the war reintensifies.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: Crowded one-way positioning makes long oil an asymmetric, contrarian portfolio hedge against a supply shock.
insights.quote: Nobody's positioned for anything but that, Patrick.

insights.claim: Polymarket prices only ~9% odds the Strait of Hormuz returns to normal by end of June and ~28% by end of July, implying real risk the disruption drags through summer.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Betting markets with real money see no imminent resolution, contradicting the consensus that a deal is days away.

insights.claim: Cushing inventories drew down again per the EIA to roughly decade-low 'tank-bottom' levels, where physical constraints create disruptions even before barrels literally run out.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Physical tightness is a real constraint that can't be financially engineered away; a catalyst could flip the narrative overnight, COVID-style.

insights.claim: Trump repeatedly signaling a deal is imminent suppresses oil prices, deters new drilling and supply response, and thereby makes any eventual price spike more violent.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: Jawboned-down prices mean no supply cushion is being built; argues for being incrementally more bullish oil every day nothing is resolved.
insights.quote: Every day that passes you should be more and more bullish oil.

insights.claim: $100 crude is the clear psychological pivot—above the recent-drop fib retracement, the moving average and prior high—that would force the media and market to re-engage with the oil narrative.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: A move into the $100 handle (still ~$10 away) is the actionable trigger to watch for an oil-narrative regime flip.
insights.quote: That's 100% the level I'm going to watch on that.

insights.claim: The ends of nearly all economic expansions (2000, 2008, European crisis) coincided with higher oil, so being long oil functions as a recession/portfolio hedge precisely when consensus is most complacent.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: Frames long oil as a tail hedge; the most dangerous setup is peak bullishness on stocks with oil ignored.
insights.quote: If you go look at the end of almost all expansions, they're met with higher oil. That's what kills it.

insights.claim: Gold has broken below its 50-week moving average for the first time since 2023, confirming a deeper mean-reversion after a two-year parabolic run, with the next major support zone at $3,600-$4,000.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: The 50-week break signals the correction has further to run; $3,600-4,000 is the level to watch for a re-entry.
insights.quote: This breakdown is the first breakdown below the 50-week moving average that gold has had since 2023.

insights.claim: The selloff is broad across precious metals—silver back near prior lows heading under $60, with platinum and palladium in fresh 'full Eiffel Tower' breakdowns to lower lows.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Confirms a sector-wide precious-metals washout, not a gold-specific issue; wait for the bloodletting to finish before buying.

insights.claim: The next major gold bull market will begin when the market flips from pricing rate hikes (SOFR ~96 / ~4% for next year) to pricing cuts amid a recognized central-bank policy error, hitting the dollar.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Gold likely stays weak for a couple more months until rate markets flip to cuts; that pivot is the macro buy signal.

insights.claim: The gold-miners sentiment index is at zero, a contrarian extreme, and buying gold while selling stocks is attractive here given gold and equities have traded as mirror images.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: Zero sentiment plus the gold-vs-stocks mirror suggests a long-gold/short-stocks switch even if gold isn't the absolute bottom.
insights.quote: Zero sentiment. Zero, buddy. Zero. That's all you have to hear.

insights.claim: Muir's macro calls (long gold/miners, bearish US dollar, long FXV) are directionally right but chronically early—often by six months or years—so traders should wait for the trend to confirm before following.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Timing caveat: even correct macro theses (long gold here) may need price confirmation before sizing up.
insights.quote: When Kevin is bullish gold here, recognize he's probably six months early.

insights.claim: Uranium equities have had a decisive head-and-shoulders breakdown, failing to reclaim the 50-day at a fib zone; the selloff is ~134 days old and over 50% from the January peak, matching prior 2024/2025 washouts (220d/-50% and 120d/-56%).
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Duration and magnitude are nearing prior washout norms, but there's still no short-term bottom signal—avoid catching the falling knife yet.
insights.quote: I'd rather be picking up a dead carcass at the bottom than try when it's still got a pulse.

insights.claim: Physical uranium is holding up while the equities collapse (UEC fell from $15 to $9 in five days), and the long-term bull case remains intact, setting up an eventual aggressive buy once the bloodletting ends.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Frames the uranium drop as a cyclical washout within a secular bull; prepare a buy list for when stocks stop bleeding.
insights.quote: I am going to be an aggressive buyer. I'm just letting the blood letting finish.

insights.claim: Muir is skeptical on uranium equities specifically, viewing it as a tough business whose stocks routinely get very cheap, so the names could flounder even if the commodity story is good.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: A caution that commodity bullishness doesn't guarantee equity returns; the stocks can stay cheap for a long time.

insights.claim: Headline CPI popped above 4% year-over-year (a nervous threshold) while core came in softer, but the inflation is a supply shock from oil with no monetary-inflation component and oil flat month-over-month.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Supply-shock inflation may soften inflation expectations if oil stays flat, easing pressure for more hikes—watch month-over-month prints.

insights.claim: SOFR futures to December 2027 price ~4% (96 handle), and both hosts agree higher oil would push headline up but ultimately roll the economy over and bring inflation lower, making the curve's hike-pricing a fade candidate.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Sets up a contrarian rates trade once the vicious downside momentum in bonds stabilizes around the 4% rate.

insights.claim: Muir bought a 96-strike Dec-2026 SOFR put financed by selling two lower-strike puts (a ratio back-spread), with breakeven around 100bps of hiking, betting even ~50bps of hikes stalls the economy.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: A concrete structure to express the 'hikes will break the economy' view with limited downside until ~75-100bps of tightening.
insights.quote: You needed more than 75 points of hiking before you even started to lose.

insights.claim: Incoming Fed chair Kevin Warsh may push to scrap the dot plots and forward guidance, returning the Fed to simply changing rates, which could steepen the yield curve.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: Removing guidance could steepen the curve and reprice risk; a structural change worth positioning for via curve steepeners.
insights.quote: Could he get rid of the dot plots?

insights.claim: Excessive Fed guidance and never surprising markets removes risk and encourages over-borrowing, and boxed the Fed in during 2022 when it delayed hiking to finish promised QE—both hosts favor eliminating guidance.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Less guidance would restore term premium and a steeper curve, changing how risk is priced across markets.

insights.claim: Financials (JP Morgan, Bank of America, Citi, Morgan Stanley) missed the entire two-month rally but are turning up in the last five days, plausibly driven by lucrative SpaceX-IPO underwriting fees.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Banks bucking the beta-1 correlation on IPO fee tailwinds is a possible rotation leader—watch for a genuine breakout vs. fakeout.
insights.quote: Lot of fees in the SpaceX.

insights.claim: Defensive sectors—healthcare (led by Eli Lilly news) and consumer staples—are turning back up after poor multi-month runs, raising the question of whether the prior immaculate defensive rotation is resuming.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Defensive/high-dividend rotation could be the destination for proceeds from chip/Mag-7 trimming; confirmation would validate the rotation-over-cascade thesis.

insights.claim: The bid in financials, healthcare and staples may just be knee-jerk reallocation of cash exiting semis/Mag-7 rather than a durable rotation signal.
insights.speaker: Kevin Muir
insights.so_what: Cautions against over-reading the defensive bounce; small-caps turning up would be the more meaningful rotation tell.
insights.quote: Doesn't go into cash. So, you're going to see everything get a little bit of a bid.

insights.claim: The current 'bipolar' market—uranium and gold slammed, rates ripping/bonds crushed, equity vol cheap until this week—is setting up potentially huge reversal trades for the second half of 2026 for those flexible enough to abandon what's been working.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: The big H2 alpha is in catching reversals (incl. in semis) rather than chasing crowded winners; flexibility is the edge.
insights.quote: You got to be flexible enough to leave the [stuff] that's been working.
